meta.title: Otzi: The Iceman
meta.author: Jan Goldberg
meta.focusQuestion: How did Otzi die, and what evidence supports the different theories?

sections.title: About 5,300 Years Ago
paragraphs: The man gently pushed aside the small brown dog yapping at his feet, tipping the basket of grain he was carrying just enough to spill. "Now see what a mess you've made." He smiled at the puppy through his irritation.
The man knew he had a lot to do to get ready for the hunt. He had no time to waste, as winter was closing in.
A tall, dark-haired boy walked over to the area of spilled grain and began scooping it up. The man looked upon his son with pride. "You must take care of things while I'm gone."
"Yes, Father. I know."
Together, father and son placed the grain back in the basket while the dog played at their heels. The boy took the grain to an underground pit where it would be protected during the upcoming winter.

The man and his son walked slowly back to their home. The man paused to stop and rub his legs. He knew the weather would soon turn colder, which would make food scarcer. Tension would be high in the community as men who weren't able to kill enough on the hunt fought over food for their families. It was important for him to leave the village soon and hunt for red deer, ibex, and wild boar. His family would need to live off the meat during the winter.
When the man returned home, he gathered his belongings together. He knew he needed to be prepared to face almost anything. Then he left his family and began his journey.

Traveling only a short distance from his home, the man killed an ibex and was carrying the meat. All of a sudden, an arrow whizzed by the man's ear. Then several more flew through the air, and one landed sharply in the back of the man's right shoulder.

The man clutched his arm in pain. Moaning, he reached behind with his left hand to withdraw the arrow but could not remove it entirely. He managed to walk slowly up a hill as he heard the men behind him taking the bundle of meat he'd dropped. Making it to the hilltop, he lay down and closed his eyes for the last time.
Little did the man know that his body would become frozen in ice and frozen in time, and that thousands of years later he would become known as Otzi, the Iceman.

Of course, this is just one possible way that Otzi, the Iceman, died. We really don't know for sure how it happened. All we can do is theorize, or make guesses based on the facts. Scientists collect facts—clues—before they make a theory. You can make a theory about how Otzi died, too. As you read the things we do know, think like a scientist and come up with your own theories.

sections.title: Finding the Iceman
paragraphs: In September 1991, two German hikers, Erika and Helmut Simon, made an amazing discovery. High in the Tyrolean Alps, at more than 10,000 feet, near the border between Austria and Italy, they found a hairless, frozen body. Only the head and part of the shoulders could be seen above the ice.

They originally thought it was the frozen corpse of a modern climber. However, a few days later, Austrian scientist Rainer Henn came up with a different theory. The corpse was yellowed and dried, so he determined that it must have been frozen for a long time. He was right. The Iceman turned out to be about 5,000 years old.

sections.title: The Man Himself
paragraphs: Scientists used carbon dating, microscopic analysis, and X-rays on the Iceman's well-preserved body to gather clues about his appearance and physical condition. They determined that Otzi was short—only about five feet tall—weighed about 110 pounds, had wavy brown hair, and probably wore a beard.
They also figured out that he lived to be about 46 years old (an elder for that time) and was bothered by several medical conditions: a bad back, arthritis, worn joints, and a bad stomach.

From Otzi's teeth, scientists were able to theorize about his diet. He probably ate mostly bread because his teeth were ground down, as they could only be from eating grains for many years, but he probably also consumed crab apples, berries, acorns, hazelnuts, and the meat of whatever animals he could hunt and kill.
Using these clues and others, scientists began to theorize how Otzi may have died.

At first, investigators thought the Iceman got caught in a storm, fell asleep, and died from the cold. But later theories seem to support the idea that the Iceman was killed. Blood was found on Otzi's clothes, and wounds were discovered on his body. A number of scientists believe that the evidence supports the theory that the Iceman died from an arrow wound to his back and knife wounds to his hands. But why? Why would someone want to kill Otzi? There are several theories.

sections.title: Theory 1: An Accident
paragraphs: While Otzi was hunting, he was accidentally shot by other hunters and then buried.
Scientists think that Otzi was hunting because of the clothing he was wearing and the equipment he was carrying when they found him frozen in the glacier.

From the many scraps of material they found (leather, fur, grass, bearskin, cowhide, tree bark) on his body, scientists think that the Iceman wore leather pants and a long-sleeved jacket or vest made of deer, goat, and ibex hide, with the fur side turned out, and possibly a long, woven grass cloak.

They guess from the clues that his shoes were waterproof and wide—seemingly designed for walking across the snow—constructed by using bearskin for the soles, cowhide or deer for the top panels, and netting made of tree bark. They think soft grass was wound around his feet to function like warm socks. Grass laces, kept dry by a leather flap, were strung through eyeholes to keep Otzi's size-six feet snug.

Scientists also theorize that the Iceman wore a hat that was made of individual cut pieces of fur, probably bearskin, sewn together. Attached to the hat were two leather straps that might have been used as a chinstrap.
These articles of clothing, scientists think, would be what one would wear going on a long trek, like a hunting expedition. This belief was reinforced by the type of equipment Otzi had with him.

Here's what scientists found: a copper ax, a bow, arrows, a quiver, and a tiny knife. Scientists think that Otzi used the bow and arrows to hunt deer and ibex, the knife to clean the animals, the ax to chop firewood, and the quiver to hold the arrows.
Otzi also carried two strips of felt and a small container made of birch bark. Scientists think he may have used the felt as tinder for starting fires and the birch-bark container to carry the felt.

Next to the container was a soft leather pouch with two pieces of flint, which were probably used for making tools and for striking sparks to start fire. A four-inch wooden stick with a tip made of deer antler may have been used for sharpening the chunks of flint.
Based on this evidence and knowledge of the time period, some scientists theorize that Otzi was hunting and may have accidentally been caught in a crossfire of arrows from other hunters.

sections.title: Theory 2: Murder for Food
paragraphs: Otzi was not killed accidentally, but was killed on purpose in a fierce battle with other hunters.

Other scientists disagree with the accidental death theory. They think that while Otzi was hunting ibex, he encountered a group of starving hunters. The hunters desperately needed a kill and did not want Otzi taking the kill from them, so they attacked him, firing arrows. Otzi fired back.

Dr. Tom Loy, director of the Queensland National Institute of Molecular Bioscience, thinks that the Iceman fired two arrows into two of his enemies, pulling his precious weapons out of their bodies each time. His theory explains why traces of blood from other people were found on Otzi's belongings: one from his knife, two from the same arrowhead, and a fourth from his coat.
In addition to his attackers shooting arrows at him, they may have attacked him with a knife. Perhaps cuts on Otzi's hands and wrist were from his attempts to defend himself.
Many scientists hold this theory to be true, but some wonder whether Otzi was killed for yet another reason.

sections.title: Theory 3: Murder for Power
paragraphs: Members of his community killed Otzi, a shaman, because he had become too powerful.

Walter Leitner of the Institute for Ancient and Early History at the University of Innsbruck in Austria thinks that the Iceman might have been a shaman. A shaman is a physical and spiritual healer who is thought to have the capability of traveling to and from the spirit world for medicine.

When scientists examined Otzi's body, they found a large number of mysterious tattoos. The tattoos included stripes, a cross, and small bars. These weren't decorations meant for others to see, for they appeared only on parts of his body that would have been hidden by clothing. Were these 57 crudely carved tattoos found on the Iceman's ankles, knees, and calves an ancient form of acupuncture? They were located on, or near, acupuncture points that would be used to treat ailments that Otzi likely suffered from—arthritis and back pain.

Some scientists think that Otzi may have worn jewelry, too. When his body was found, a leather string with a fringe, strung through the two-inch disk made of white stone lay nearby. Could this mysterious object have been worn by a medicine man, like Otzi, as a necklace?

Near Otzi's body, researchers also found two mushrooms on a piece of leather. Scientists know that this kind of mushroom can be used to fight sickness. Was Otzi carrying them as medicine—something the hunters needed? Was the Iceman really a shaman?

sections.title: A Secret Kept
paragraphs: These are just a few of the scientific theories about the Iceman. They change when the facts—the clues—change. But sometimes the clues remain the same and the scientists reinterpret the facts differently. How do you interpret the facts? What kinds of theories do you have now from the facts about how Otzi may have died?
The Iceman could have died in a vast number of possible ways. For now, though, he is still keeping the true story a secret from us.

vocabulary.definition: using chemicals to determine the age of something
vocabulary.sentence: Scientists used carbon dating, microscopic analysis, and X-rays on the Iceman's well-preserved body to gather clues about his appearance and physical condition.

options: a method for finding underground water sources
a technique for preserving ancient food
a way to determine the age of something using chemicals
a tool for mapping ancient glaciers

vocabulary.sentence: They originally thought it was the frozen corpse of a modern climber.

options: a living person found in a remote area
only bones without any skin or tissue

vocabulary.definition: information that can prove something true or false
vocabulary.sentence: A number of scientists believe that the evidence supports the theory that the Iceman died from an arrow wound to his back and knife wounds to his hands.

options: information that can prove something true or false
a wild guess without any facts
a personal belief that cannot be tested

vocabulary.definition: a large accumulation of ice and compacted snow in a mountain valley
vocabulary.sentence: Scientists think that Otzi was hunting because of the clothing he was wearing and the equipment he was carrying when they found him frozen in the glacier.

options: a large accumulation of ice and compacted snow in a mountain valley
a river of hot lava flowing from a volcano
a tall sand dune in a desert
a frozen ocean full of fish

vocabulary.sentence: Scientists used carbon dating, microscopic analysis, and X-rays on the Iceman's well-preserved body to gather clues about his appearance and physical condition.
All we can do is theorize, or make guesses based on the facts. Scientists collect facts—clues—before they make a theory.

options: a proven fact that cannot be changed
a random guess with no facts behind it
a solution that is always certain
a possible explanation for some event

vocabulary.definition: a treatment where needles are inserted into the skin at specific locations on the body to relieve pain
vocabulary.sentence: Were these 57 crudely carved tattoos found on the Iceman's ankles, knees, and calves an ancient form of acupuncture?

options: a treatment where needles are inserted into the skin to relieve pain
a type of surgery used in ancient times
herbal medicine made from mountain plants
a form of meditation practiced by shamans

vocabulary.definition: a disease in which one's joints swell and stiffen
vocabulary.sentence: They also figured out that he lived to be about 46 years old (an elder for that time) and was bothered by several medical conditions: a bad back, arthritis, worn joints, and a bad stomach.

options: a disease that causes severe headaches
a disease in which one's joints swell and stiffen
a type of fever caused by cold weather
a stomach condition from eating bad food

vocabulary.sentence: A shaman is a physical and spiritual healer who is thought to have the capability of traveling to and from the spirit world for medicine.

options: a warrior who leads the community in battle
a farmer who grows food for the village
a hunter who provides meat for the community

vocabulary.definition: permanent marks made on the body with ink
vocabulary.sentence: When scientists examined Otzi's body, they found a large number of mysterious tattoos.

options: permanent marks made on the body with ink
decorations painted on the skin temporarily

vocabulary.definition: a strained state or feeling; stress
vocabulary.sentence: Tension would be high in the community as men who weren't able to kill enough on the hunt fought over food for their families.

options: a feeling of stress or strain

vocabulary.definition: injuries to the body, such as cuts or holes made by weapons
vocabulary.sentence: Blood was found on Otzi's clothes, and wounds were discovered on his body.

options: injuries to the body, such as cuts or breaks in the skin
tools used for healing the sick

questions.question: What do scientists think killed Otzi?
questions.evidence: A number of scientists believe that the evidence supports the theory that the Iceman died from an arrow wound to his back and knife wounds to his hands.
choices: A rock fall from the mountain
An arrow wound to his back and knife wounds to his hands
Exposure to extreme cold in a storm
A fall from a high cliff
questions.explanation: Scientists found blood on Otzi's clothes and wounds on his body. The evidence points to an arrow wound in his back and knife wounds on his hands.

questions.question: What is a theory?
questions.evidence: All we can do is theorize, or make guesses based on the facts. Scientists collect facts—clues—before they make a theory.
choices: a solution that is certain
a way to solve a mystery
a possible reason for some event
an explanation that is only based on guesses
questions.explanation: A theory is a possible explanation based on facts and clues. It is not just a random guess—scientists collect evidence before forming a theory.

questions.question: In the section titled "Finding the Iceman," what is the main point the author is making?
questions.evidence: In September 1991, two German hikers, Erika and Helmut Simon, made an amazing discovery. High in the Tyrolean Alps, at more than 10,000 feet, near the border between Austria and Italy, they found a hairless, frozen body.
choices: People looked for a long time for the Iceman.
Two hikers in the Alps found the Iceman in 1991.
Other hunters probably killed the Iceman.
After the Iceman was found, scientists took the body to their labs to study.
questions.explanation: The main point of "Finding the Iceman" is that two hikers made an amazing discovery—they found a frozen body high in the Alps in 1991.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the Iceman?
questions.evidence: They determined that Otzi was short—only about five feet tall—weighed about 110 pounds, had wavy brown hair, and probably wore a beard. They also figured out that he lived to be about 46 years old (an elder for that time) and was bothered by several medical conditions: a bad back, arthritis, worn joints, and a bad stomach.
choices: about five feet tall and 110 pounds
wavy brown hair and probably wore a beard
All of the above
about forty-six years old and had medical conditions
questions.explanation: All of these details describe Otzi. He was about five feet tall, weighed 110 pounds, had wavy brown hair, probably wore a beard, was about 46, and had several medical conditions including arthritis.

questions.question: Why do scientists think the Iceman ate a lot of bread?
questions.evidence: He probably ate mostly bread because his teeth were ground down, as they could only be from eating grains for many years.
choices: His teeth were worn down.
He had flour in his stomach.
They found grains of wheat in his sack.
Loaves of bread were found in ancient houses.
questions.explanation: Scientists concluded from Otzi's teeth that he ate mostly bread because his teeth were ground down from eating grains for many years.

questions.question: Why did the author include three different theories for how Otzi died?
questions.evidence: All we can do is theorize, or make guesses based on the facts. Scientists collect facts—clues—before they make a theory. You can make a theory about how Otzi died, too. As you read the things we do know, think like a scientist and come up with your own theories.
choices: so readers could pick their favorite one
to prove to people in the area that Otzi was found there
to show readers how scientists base their theories on facts
to explain how scientists came to decide the best of the three
questions.explanation: The author presents three theories to show how scientists use evidence and facts to form different explanations. Readers are encouraged to think like scientists and form their own theories.

questions.question: What is evidence?
questions.evidence: Scientists collect facts—clues—before they make a theory.
choices: something unknown
a way to test a theory
ancient bones of a human
facts or information that help prove something
questions.explanation: Evidence is facts or information that can prove something true or false. Scientists collect evidence (clues) before forming theories.

questions.question: What support do scientists have for the theory of Otzi being murdered?
questions.evidence: Blood was found on Otzi's clothes, and wounds were discovered on his body. A number of scientists believe that the evidence supports the theory that the Iceman died from an arrow wound to his back and knife wounds to his hands.
choices: He was found at over ten thousand feet in elevation.
He had wounds and blood on his clothes.
His skin was yellowed and dried.
He was about five feet tall and weighed 110 pounds.
questions.explanation: Scientists found blood on Otzi's clothes and wounds on his body. This evidence supports the theory that he was murdered rather than dying from natural causes.

questions.question: Why do scientists think Otzi had been hunting when he was killed?
questions.evidence: Scientists think that Otzi was hunting because of the clothing he was wearing and the equipment he was carrying when they found him frozen in the glacier.
choices: They saw blood on his arrow tips.
They could tell he was a strong man from the bow he carried.
A shaman from Austria analyzed Otzi and found he was about five thousand years old.
They found equipment and clothing that would have been taken on a hunting trip.
questions.explanation: Scientists believe Otzi was hunting because of the clothing (leather pants, fur jacket, waterproof shoes) and equipment (copper ax, bow, arrows, knife) he was carrying—all items needed for a hunting expedition.

questions.question: What seems odd to scientists about the tattoos they found on Otzi?
questions.evidence: These weren't decorations meant for others to see, for they appeared only on parts of his body that would have been hidden by clothing.
choices: They were hidden by his clothes.
They were made of charcoal and not ink.
They were only found on his hands.
They were clearly decorations for others to admire.
questions.explanation: What's odd is that the 57 tattoos were located on parts of Otzi's body that would be hidden by clothing—ankles, knees, and calves. They weren't decorations for others to see, which led scientists to think they might have been an ancient form of acupuncture.

questions.question: Which of the three theories of Otzi's death do you think is probably true? Support your answer with at least two pieces of evidence from the text.
questions.explanation: Use evidence from the text to support your choice. Consider: the blood on Otzi's belongings, the arrow wound in his back, the cuts on his hands, the tattoos, and the equipment found with him.
opinionPrompts: Which theory do you think best explains how Otzi died?
What two pieces of evidence from the text support your choice?
Why do the other theories seem less likely to you?

evidence.title: The Arrow Wound
evidence.context: Scientists examined Otzi's body and found critical evidence about how he died. An x-ray revealed an arrowhead lodged in his shoulder.

claim: Otzi died from a knife wound to his hands
Otzi died from an arrow wound to his back
Otzi died because he fell asleep in a storm
Otzi died from a fall off a mountain
evidence: A copper ax was found with his belongings
Traces of blood from two different people were on his arrow
An arrowhead was found in Otzi's right shoulder
His shoes were designed for walking in snow
reasoning: The arrow in his shoulder proves he was in a violent encounter and could not have survived, supporting the theory that he was killed.
The arrow wound shows that Otzi was a hunter who was practicing his aim.
The arrow proves that Otzi died from natural causes related to the cold weather.
The arrow wound was accidentally caused by another hunter during a hunt.

evidence.title: Blood on Otzi's Belongings
evidence.context: Dr. Tom Loy analyzed items found with Otzi and discovered traces of blood from multiple people on his equipment.

claim: Otzi was involved in a violent confrontation with other people before his death
Otzi was a peaceful farmer who never fought anyone
The blood on Otzi's belongings was all his own
Otzi's belongings were never tested for blood
evidence: His copper ax was brand new and unused
Traces of blood from at least four different people were found on his knife, arrowhead, and coat
Only animal blood was found on his tools
His bow had never been strung or used
reasoning: The blood on multiple items shows Otzi was a skilled hunter who hunted many animals.
The blood evidence proves that Otzi was attacked by a large army.
Blood from multiple people on different items shows Otzi fought with others, supporting the murder theory.
The blood was placed there by modern scientists who handled the body.

evidence.title: The Mysterious Tattoos
evidence.context: Scientists found 57 tattoos on Otzi's body. The tattoos included stripes, a cross, and small bars located on his ankles, knees, and calves.

claim: The tattoos were purely decorative, made for others to admire
The tattoos may have been an ancient form of acupuncture to treat Otzi's pain
The tattoos were marks of a criminal in Otzi's community
The tattoos were made after Otzi died
evidence: The tattoos were located on body parts hidden by clothing
All 57 tattoos were found on Otzi's face and hands
The tattoos were made with modern ink
Otzi had no medical conditions that needed treatment
reasoning: The tattoos being hidden by clothing suggests they were not decorations but served a medical purpose, possibly acupuncture to treat Otzi's arthritis and back pain.
Since the tattoos were hidden, they must have been made by someone who didn't know Otzi well.
The hidden location of the tattoos proves they were decorations for a religious ceremony.
The tattoos show that Otzi was a warrior who earned marks in battle.

summarizeActivities.title: About 5,300 Years Ago
summarizeActivities.instructions: Summarize what happens to the man in the opening story. Include: what he was preparing for, what happened during his journey, and how he died.
sentenceStarters: The man was preparing to go on a hunt because...
keyPoints: The man was preparing for a winter hunt
He stored grain for his family
An arrow struck him in the shoulder
He made it to a hilltop and died
His body froze in the ice for thousands of years

summarizeActivities.title: Finding the Iceman & The Man Himself
summarizeActivities.instructions: Summarize how Otzi was discovered and what scientists learned about him from studying his body.
sentenceStarters: Scientists used carbon dating and X-rays to discover...
From his teeth, scientists learned that...
keyPoints: Two German hikers found a frozen body in the Alps in 1991
Scientists determined the body was about 5,000 years old
Otzi was about 5 feet tall, 110 pounds, with brown hair and a beard
He was about 46 years old and had arthritis, bad back, and worn joints
He probably ate mostly bread based on his worn-down teeth
Blood and wounds on his body suggest he was killed

summarizeActivities.title: The Three Theories
summarizeActivities.instructions: Summarize the three theories scientists have about how Otzi died. For each theory, include the main idea and one piece of evidence.
sentenceStarters: The first theory says Otzi died because...
keyPoints: Theory 1 (Accident): Otzi was accidentally shot by other hunters while hunting
Evidence: He was wearing hunting clothing and carried hunting equipment
Theory 2 (Murder for Food): Starving hunters killed Otzi to protect their food supply
Evidence: Blood from other people was found on his knife, arrow, and coat
Theory 3 (Murder for Power): Otzi may have been a shaman killed by his community
Evidence: His 57 tattoos may have been ancient acupuncture

summarizeActivities.title: A Secret Kept
summarizeActivities.instructions: Summarize the author's conclusion. What does the author want readers to understand about the theories and the evidence?
sentenceStarters: The author explains that the theories change when...
The author wants readers to understand that...
keyPoints: Theories change when new facts and clues are discovered
Sometimes the same clues lead to different interpretations
Readers are encouraged to think like scientists and form their own theories
Otzi is still keeping the true story a secret

grammar.title: Adverbs
grammar.explanation: Adverbs describe verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. They tell HOW, WHEN, WHERE, or HOW OFTEN something happens. Many adverbs end in -ly.
rules: Most adverbs are formed by adding -ly to an adjective: gentle -> gently, slow -> slowly
Some adverbs don't end in -ly: soon, very, often, always, never
Adverbs answer the questions: How? When? Where? How often? To what degree?

exercises.sentence: The man gently pushed aside the small brown dog yapping at his feet.
exercises.hint: Which word tells HOW the man pushed the dog?

exercises.sentence: The man and his son walked slowly back to their home.
exercises.hint: Which word tells HOW they walked?

exercises.sentence: One landed sharply in the back of the man's right shoulder.
exercises.hint: Which word tells HOW the arrow landed?

exercises.sentence: They originally thought it was the frozen corpse of a modern climber.
exercises.hint: Which word tells WHEN they thought it was a modern climber?

exercises.sentence: The hunters desperately needed a kill and did not want Otzi taking the kill from them.
exercises.hint: Which word tells HOW much the hunters needed a kill?

exercises.question: Which word is the adverb in this sentence?
exercises.sentence: He managed to walk slowly up a hill.
exercises.explanation: 'Slowly' is an adverb that tells HOW he walked up the hill.

exercises.question: Which word is the adverb in this sentence?
exercises.sentence: All of a sudden, an arrow whizzed by the man's ear.
exercises.explanation: In this sentence, 'sudden' functions as part of the adverbial phrase 'all of a sudden,' telling WHEN the arrow whizzed by.

exercises.question: What does an adverb describe?
exercises.explanation: Adverbs describe verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. They tell how, when, where, or how often something happens.

exercises.sentence: The man _____ (gentle/gently) pushed aside the small brown dog.
exercises.explanation: 'Gently' is the adverb form. It tells HOW the man pushed the dog.

exercises.sentence: The arrow landed _____ (sharp/sharply) in the man's shoulder.
exercises.explanation: 'Sharply' is the adverb form. It tells HOW the arrow landed.
'Desperately' is the adverb form. It tells HOW MUCH the hunters needed a kill.

prefixExercises.title: Prefixes dis- and re-
prefixExercises.explanation: Prefixes are added to the beginning of words to change their meaning. The prefix dis- means 'not' or 'the opposite of.' The prefix re- means 'again' or 'back.'

disExamples.sentence: The scientists disagree about how Otzi died.
The truth about Otzi's death has not yet disappeared from hope.
Two hikers discover the frozen body in the Alps.
The results of the carbon dating did not disappoint the scientists.

reExamples.sentence: Scientists reuse advanced technology to study ancient remains.

reExamples.definition: to turn back or go back
reExamples.sentence: When the man returned home, he gathered his belongings together.
Scientists rebuild a picture of Otzi's life from the evidence.
Scientists reexamine the evidence when new technology becomes available.

exercises.instructions: Add the prefix dis- or re- to the base word to complete the sentence.

items.sentence: Scientists _____ about how Otzi died—some think it was an accident, others think it was murder.
Scientists _____ the tattoos when new evidence became available.
Two hikers _____ a frozen body high in the Alps.
When the man _____ home, he gathered his belongings.

exercises.question: What does the prefix dis- mean?
exercises.explanation: The prefix dis- means 'not' or 'the opposite of.' For example, disagree means 'not agree.'

exercises.question: What does the prefix re- mean?
exercises.explanation: The prefix re- means 'again' or 'back.' For example, return means 'to turn back' and reuse means 'to use again.'

exercises.question: Which word has the prefix dis-?
exercises.explanation: 'Discover' has the prefix dis- (dis + cover). It means to find or uncover something.

intro.title: Theory vs. Fact
intro.content: A THEORY is a possible explanation based on facts. It is NOT a random guess! Scientists collect evidence (clues) before making a theory.

intro.title: Tension
intro.content: Tension means stress or strain. In the story, tension was high because food was scarce and men worried about feeding their families in winter.

intro.title: Adverb: Gently
intro.content: "The man gently pushed aside the small brown dog." GENTLY is an adverb telling HOW the man pushed the dog. It comes from the adjective 'gentle.'

intro.title: Think Like a Scientist
intro.content: The author wants YOU to think like a scientist! As you read, collect facts (clues) and form your own theory about how Otzi died.

finding.title: Corpse
finding.content: A corpse is a dead body. The hikers first thought they found the corpse of a modern climber—but it was actually 5,000 years old!

finding.title: Carbon Dating
finding.content: Carbon dating uses chemicals to determine how old something is. This is how scientists knew Otzi was about 5,000 years old!

finding.title: A Surprising Discovery
finding.content: The hikers thought it was a MODERN body, but scientist Rainer Henn noticed the corpse was yellowed and dried—meaning it was VERY old! This shows why evidence matters.

manHimself.title: Preserved
manHimself.content: Preserved means kept in the same condition. Otzi's body was well-preserved in the glacier for 5,000 years! The ice acted like a natural freezer.

manHimself.title: Arthritis
manHimself.content: Arthritis is a disease where joints swell and stiffen. Otzi suffered from arthritis and back pain—even 5,300 years ago, people had these conditions!

manHimself.title: Evidence & Wounds
manHimself.content: Evidence = facts that prove something. Blood on Otzi's clothes and wounds on his body are key evidence that he was killed, not that he just froze to death.

manHimself.title: Adverb: Sharply
manHimself.content: "One landed sharply in the back of the man's right shoulder." SHARPLY is an adverb telling HOW the arrow landed. It comes from the adjective 'sharp.'

manHimself.title: What Scientists Found
manHimself.content: Key evidence: 1) Arrow wound to back, 2) Knife wounds to hands, 3) Blood on clothes from other people, 4) 57 tattoos, 5) Hunting equipment. Each clue supports a different theory!

theory1.title: Glacier
theory1.content: A glacier is a large mass of ice and snow in a mountain valley. Otzi was found frozen in a glacier—the ice preserved his body for 5,000 years!

theory1.title: Hunting Evidence
theory1.content: Scientists think Otzi was hunting because of what he wore and carried: leather pants, waterproof shoes, copper ax, bow, arrows, knife. These are all items for a HUNTING expedition!

theory1.title: Prefix: dis-
theory1.content: Scientists DISAGREE about how Otzi died. The prefix DIS- means 'not' or 'the opposite of.' DIS + agree = disagree (to not agree).

theory2.title: Dr. Tom Loy's Evidence
theory2.content: Dr. Tom Loy found blood from 4 different people on Otzi's belongings: knife, arrowhead (2 people), and coat. This supports the theory that Otzi FOUGHT with other hunters!

theory2.title: Adverb: Desperately
theory2.content: "The hunters desperately needed a kill." DESPERATELY is an adverb telling HOW MUCH the hunters needed food. It comes from the adjective 'desperate.'

theory2.title: Prefix: re-
theory2.content: When the man RETURNED home... The prefix RE- means 'again' or 'back.' RE + turn = return (to turn back/go back).

theory3.title: Shaman
theory3.content: A shaman is a spiritual and physical healer who travels to the spirit world for medicine. Some scientists think Otzi was a shaman!

theory3.title: Tattoos & Acupuncture
theory3.content: Otzi had 57 tattoos on body parts HIDDEN by clothing—not for decoration! They were near acupuncture points for his arthritis and back pain. Could tattoos have been ancient medical treatment?

theory3.title: The Shaman Theory
theory3.content: Evidence Otzi might be a shaman: 1) 57 tattoos near acupuncture points, 2) Mushrooms that fight sickness, 3) A mysterious stone disk that might be a necklace. But is this enough evidence?

secretKept.title: Your Turn to Theorize!
secretKept.content: The author says theories CHANGE when facts change. Sometimes the SAME clues lead to DIFFERENT interpretations. What is YOUR theory? Use at least 2 pieces of evidence to support it!
